title: The nonpareil pattern, raking pigments on carrageenan size
subtitle: How to produce the fine-stripe base pattern for classical European marbling
excerpt: The nonpareil is the most fundamental combed pattern in Western carrageenan-bath marbling: a wide-tooth rake followed by a fine-tooth comb through floating pigment drops produces a field of tight parallel zigzags. It is the base from which the French curl, bouquet, and shell patterns are all derived.
sourceNotes: C. W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881): Woolnough describes the nonpareil as the most fundamental of the European combing patterns and provides detailed tooth-spacing guidance and pigment-loading advice. Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893) provides supporting detail on comb speed and pattern variation.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard fine-stripe combed pattern in European carrageenan-bath marbling. A wide-tooth rake followed by a fine-tooth comb produces alternating zigzag stripes across the full tray.
The carrageenan-water solution in the marbling tray. Pigments float on the size surface and are manipulated with rakes and combs before the paper picks them up.

content.text: is the first pattern most marblers learn and the base for nearly all classical European combing patterns. The technique requires two comb passes across the
at perpendicular angles. The difficulty is not in the motion but in maintaining a constant pace, any pause produces a visible wider section in the finished stripe.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry safety
attrs.body: Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying it to paper and rinse splashes promptly. Carrageenan is non-toxic but spilled size is very slippery, wipe spills immediately. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

items.name: Carrageenan (marbling size)
items.notes: Mixed 24 hours in advance at 1.5-2% carrageenan to water by weight. Allow air bubbles to settle before use.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid
items.notes: Three to five colours, each tested for spread on the size. The colours should push each other out cleanly without sinking.

items.name: Ox-gall (marbling)
items.notes: Added to each colour to control spread. More ox-gall produces a larger circle on the surface; less produces a smaller, denser one.

items.name: Alum, potash (mordant)
items.notes: Applied to the paper as a 30 g per litre solution. Must be fully dry before the sheet contacts the size.

items.name: Marbling tray
items.notes: At least the size of the sheet being marbled. A tray with low sides lets the comb clear the edge cleanly.

items.name: Ebru comb set
items.notes: You need two tooth spacings: a wide-tooth rake (15-20 mm) for the first pass and a fine-tooth comb (3-5 mm) for the nonpareil pass.

content.text: Drop the pigments in horizontal rows of alternating colours across the size surface. Each drop pushes adjacent drops outward, building up a pattern of concentric rings. Once the surface is fully covered, draw the wide-tooth rake from left to right across the full tray in a single, even pass. This breaks the circular drops into an initial set of stripes.
Draw the fine-tooth comb from the top of the tray to the bottom, perpendicular to the first rake direction. This crosses and subdivides the stripes into the characteristic tight

zigzag. For a full nonpareil, follow immediately with a second fine-comb pass from bottom to top. Speed must be constant from edge to edge of the tray: any pause leaves a wider section where the pigment had time to flow before being caught.

Hold the alum-treated sheet by its two short edges. Lower the centre of the sheet onto the size first, then release both edges simultaneously to avoid trapping air. Allow ten to fifteen seconds of contact. Lift one long edge and peel the sheet toward you in a single smooth motion. Rinse under running water immediately and lay flat to dry.

items.solution: The ox-gall ratio is too low or the size is too thin. Add one more drop of ox-gall to the colour and test on the size surface before proceeding.

items.problem: Stripes are uneven width across the sheet
items.solution: The comb passed was at varying speeds. Practise the pass on a dry tray surface to establish a consistent rhythm before loading with pigment.

items.problem: Paper lifts unevenly and the pattern smears
items.solution: Air was trapped under the sheet at lay-down. Work slowly from the centre outward when lowering the sheet, or roll it down one end to the other.
